steps.title: Pin down the claim
steps.content: The underlined sentence says "Thanks to ‘Aha Pūnana Leo, the number of young people who speak the language has increased." Support must show (1) a rise in young Hawaiian speakers and (2) a connection to ‘Aha Pūnana Leo.

steps.title: Find the supporting evidence
steps.content: Choice A provides a then-and-now count tied directly to the school: fewer than fifty children spoke Hawaiian at founding, versus more than 2,000 students at ‘Aha Pūnana Leo today. That is exactly the increase the claim asserts.

steps.title: Eliminate the others
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Fewer than fifty children... now more than 2,000 students at ‘Aha Pūnana Leo speak it': shows both the increase and its tie to the school. ✓
  • Choice B — '680,000 Native Hawaiian people... half... lived in Hawai‘i': a population statistic that says nothing about young Hawaiian speakers or the school. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Hawaiian is very similar to... Tahitian, Samoan, and Māori': linguistic kinship is irrelevant to whether speaker numbers rose. ✗
  • Choice D — 'tens of thousands... including over 37,000 Samoans': about other Pacific Islander communities, not Hawaiian-speaking youth. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
